skill.name: container-hadolint
skill.description: Dockerfile security linting and best practice validation using Hadolint. The skill helps review Dockerfiles, configure Hadolint rules, and add CI checks for container security.
skill.summary: Review Dockerfiles with Hadolint security rules and CI guidance.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many severe patterns, but review shows most are Markdown examples or Hadolint configuration references, not hidden malicious behavior. The skill is safe to publish with a warning because it encourages external command execution and includes an unverified network installer pattern.

medium_findings.title: Unverified Network Download in CI Template
medium_findings.description: The GitHub Actions template downloads the latest Hadolint binary with wget and makes it executable without checksum verification or immutable version pinning. This is legitimate setup guidance, but it creates supply-chain risk if copied directly into production CI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network download is explicit and no checksum or fixed release asset is shown. The context is an installation example, so the risk is supply-chain exposure rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Agent May Run External Container Linting Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill is built around running hadolint, docker, find, jq, and CI shell snippets against repository files. This is expected for the skill, but users should understand that following the instructions can execute local tools and read Dockerfiles in the workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly part of Dockerfile linting workflows and arguments are generally quoted. The risk depends on whether an AI agent executes the examples automatically in an untrusted repository.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Examples Triggered External Command Alerts
low_findings.description: Most external command findings occur inside Markdown examples that demonstrate Hadolint usage, CI scripts, or Dockerfile remediation patterns. They are not hidden runtime code bundled with the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced examples and rule documentation. I did not find evidence that the skill ships executable scripts that run these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Recursive Delete Alerts Are Dockerfile Cleanup Examples
low_findings.description: The recursive deletion findings are examples of removing apt package lists or apk cache inside Dockerfile instructions. They do not delete host root or home directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are package-manager cache directories in Dockerfile examples. They are bounded cleanup commands, not broad deletion of / or user home paths.

low_findings.title: Environment Secret Alerts Are Secure BuildKit Examples
low_findings.description: The API key and authorization examples appear in documentation that warns against ARG secrets and recommends Docker BuildKit secret mounts. No evidence found of credential collection or exfiltration by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text is defensive guidance about avoiding secrets in image layers. The external URL is an example endpoint, not a hidden destination used by the skill.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance and C2 Keyword Alerts Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: Network and system reconnaissance alerts map to security-framework references, trusted registry examples, label schema fields, or ShellCheck rule names. No evidence found of scanning networks or contacting command-and-control infrastructure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens appear in normal security documentation and Hadolint configuration. I found no semantic evidence of malicious remote-control behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unpinned External Installer Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes install examples that download or pull current tool versions from the network. Prefer pinned versions, checksums, or package-manager trust controls when adopting the templates.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The templates use latest-style downloads or image tags. This is common setup guidance, but immutable pinning would reduce supply-chain risk.

content.value_statement: Dockerfiles often hide insecure defaults, unpinned packages, and weak build patterns. This skill guides Hadolint scans, CI integration, and practical remediation for container builds.
actual_capabilities: Explain Hadolint findings and map them to Dockerfile security practices.
Generate local Hadolint commands for single or multiple Dockerfiles.
Recommend strict, balanced, or permissive Hadolint configuration profiles.
Provide GitHub Actions and GitLab CI patterns for Dockerfile linting.
Suggest remediation for common Dockerfile issues such as root users, ADD usage, and unpinned packages.
Help teams document Dockerfile linting results for security review.
limitations: It does not replace container image vulnerability scanning tools such as Trivy or Grype.
It cannot prove that a built image is secure after runtime dependencies are installed.
CI examples may need version pinning, checksum verification, and local policy adjustments.
Hadolint rule results can require human review for legacy or specialized Dockerfiles.

use_cases.title: Developer Dockerfile Review
use_cases.description: Check a Dockerfile before opening a pull request and get clear remediation steps for common Hadolint findings.

use_cases.title: CI Policy Rollout
use_cases.description: Add Hadolint to GitHub Actions or GitLab CI with thresholds that fit production and migration workflows.

use_cases.title: Container Security Baseline
use_cases.description: Create a Dockerfile linting baseline aligned with trusted registries, least privilege, and CIS Docker guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Scan One Dockerfile
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Dockerfile with Hadolint guidance. Explain the most important security issues and suggest safe fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a CI Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add Hadolint to CI for this repository. Use a warning threshold and explain any required permissions.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Rule Severity
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Hadolint configuration for production Dockerfiles. Separate required security rules from advisory style rules.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Multiple Services
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Hadolint audit for all Dockerfiles in this monorepo. Group findings by risk and propose a phased remediation plan.

output_examples.input: A Dockerfile uses ubuntu:latest, installs curl without a pinned version, and runs as root.
output_examples.output: The review highlights mutable base images, unpinned packages, and missing least-privilege controls. It recommends a fixed base tag, pinned package versions, and a non-root user.

output_examples.input: A team wants GitHub Actions to fail when Dockerfiles violate important Hadolint rules.
output_examples.output: The response proposes a Hadolint workflow, explains the SARIF upload option, and warns to pin tool versions or verify downloads.

output_examples.input: A legacy service has many Hadolint warnings and cannot fix all issues at once.
output_examples.output: The response suggests a permissive migration profile, documents accepted temporary ignores, and defines stricter gates for production images.

best_practices: Run Hadolint before image builds so Dockerfile defects fail early.
Pin base images, packages, actions, and downloaded tools wherever possible.
Record rule suppressions with a reason and an owner for later cleanup.
anti_patterns: Do not copy network installer examples into production CI without checksum or version controls.
Do not suppress broad Hadolint rule groups to make a pipeline pass quickly.
Do not treat Dockerfile linting as a replacement for runtime image scanning.

faq.question: Does this skill run Hadolint automatically?
faq.answer: It provides commands and workflow guidance. An agent or user must decide whether to run those commands in the workspace.

faq.question: Can it scan built container images?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on Dockerfile linting. Use image scanners for installed packages and runtime vulnerabilities.

faq.question: Is it suitable for CI pipelines?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes GitHub Actions and GitLab CI patterns, but teams should pin versions and verify downloads.

faq.question: Can it help with CIS Docker Benchmark alignment?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains Hadolint rules that support common CIS Docker Benchmark practices.

faq.question: How should teams handle false positives?
faq.answer: Use targeted rule suppressions with written justifications. Avoid global ignores unless they are temporary and tracked.

faq.question: What permissions are needed?
faq.answer: Local scans need read access to Dockerfiles. CI workflows may need artifact upload or security event permissions.
